news_summary: Recent discoveries in space exploration and astronomy include new insights into ancient asteroids from meteorite studies, advancements in lunar landing technology through Artemis III testing, and the identification of previously unseen nearby white dwarf stars.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les récentes découvertes en matière d'exploration spatiale et d'astronomie, on peut citer de nouvelles connaissances sur les astéroïdes anciens issues de l'étude des météorites, les progrès réalisés dans la technologie d'alunissage grâce aux essais de la mission Artemis III, ainsi que l'identification de naines blanches proches jusqu'alors inconnues.
news_summary_es: Entre los descubrimientos recientes en el ámbito de la exploración espacial y la astronomía se incluyen nuevos conocimientos sobre asteroides antiguos a partir del estudio de meteoritos, avances en la tecnología de alunizaje gracias a las pruebas de Artemis III y la identificación de estrellas enanas blancas cercanas que hasta ahora no se habían observado.

articles.title: NASA Study of Pristine Meteorite Adds to Story of Ancient Asteroids
articles.summary: A meteorite recovered immediately upon its fall to Earth on July 16, 2024, is helping NASA scientists uncover new clues about ancient water, the chemical evolution of primitive asteroids, and the ingredients that may have helped make life possible throughout the early solar system.
articles.content:
C1 clasts in Hillsborough: On the left is a back-scattered electron image with two C1 748 clasts circled. On the right, an X-ray map of the same area as (A), indicating Na enrichment in 749 of the C1 clasts relative to the bulk of Hillsborough. Credit: NASA/SETI

A meteorite recovered immediately upon its fall to Earth on July 16, 2024, is helping NASA scientists uncover new clues about ancient water, the chemical evolution of primitive asteroids, and the ingredients that may have helped make life possible throughout the early solar system.

This rapid recovery began when an amateur astronomer in New Jersey quickly recognized that a newly fallen meteorite had landed on his property. Recognizing its scientific value and wearing protective gloves, he collected the fragments and stored them in aluminum foil and glass containers, which preserved delicate minerals and organic compounds that are often altered by moisture, weather, and contamination.

As the meteorite fell to Earth, cameras across New Jersey captured its fiery passage through the atmosphere. Scientists used these observations to reconstruct the fireball's trajectory and, after recovering the meteorite, combined this data with laboratory analyses to determine where in the solar system the rock most likely originated. In a study published Wednesday in the journal Science Advances, researchers found evidence that ancient salty water altered minerals within the meteorite's parent asteroid, preserving unique minerals and a rich inventory of organic compounds.

"When we have both a documented fireball and a quick recovery of its meteorite, we can learn not only what the rock is made of, but where it came from in the asteroid belt," said Peter Jenniskens, meteor astronomer at both NASA's Ames Research Center in California's Silicon Valley and the SETI Institute, and lead author of the study.

Combined radar detections from the Hillsborough meteorite fall. The green line shows the fireball’s projected path, while colored radar signatures show falling meteorite fragments drifting east-northeast with prevailing winds. Credit: NASA/Marc Fries

Named for the township where it was recovered, the Hillsborough meteorite belongs to a class of carbon-rich meteorites known as CM carbonaceous chondrites. These primitive rocks preserve some of the oldest materials in the solar system, recording the chemical processes that shaped asteroids more than 4.5 billion years ago.

While examining the unusually pristine meteorite, researchers found a mosaic of tiny broken-up rocks and noticed that some contained unusually high concentrations of sodium — an unexpected finding for this type of meteorite. The surprising signal prompted a closer investigation using powerful electron microscopes that allowed scientists to examine the meteorite from the millimeter scale down to individual atoms. By combining observations across multiple scales, researchers reconstructed the history of the minerals and the fluids that once flowed through them.

These analyses revealed microscopic fractures filled with sodium-rich material left behind by ancient brines. Unlike pure water, brines contain dissolved salts that allow them to transport elements and chemically alter the rocks they move through. In the case of the Hillsborough sample, those ancient fluids altered the asteroid's minerals and left behind chemical evidence that remained preserved for billions of years.

Scientists were also able to detect fragile sodium-carbonate salts that normally react with moisture in Earth's atmosphere before they can be studied. Jangmi Han, a paper co-author and mineralogist at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, identified evidence of ancient brines preserved within microscopic fractures. Similar salts were identified in samples returned from the asteroids Bennu and Ryugu by NASA's OSIRIS-REx mission and JAXA's (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) Hayabusa2 mission. However,Hillsborough marks the first time the salts have been identified in a CM carbonaceous chondrite meteorite, offering a new glimpse into the surfaces of the primitive asteroids that produced these meteorites.

Together, these findings suggest that ancient, salt-rich brines were more widespread among primitive asteroids than previously recognized, and provide scientists with new opportunities to compare how water altered different asteroid bodies across the early solar system.

"The chips of the most salt-rich bits of this meteorite are quite comparable to the samples returned by the Hayabusa2 and OSIRIS-REx missions," said Mike Zolensky, a meteorite researcher at NASA Johnson and co-author of the study. "They're not identical. They're different in some very interesting ways, but they've seen very similar processes."

Following the history of water through the solar system is an essential part of understanding the origin of life. Mike Zolensky Meteorite Researcher

Scientists expected Hillsborough to contain a rich suite of organic compounds because it is a CM carbonaceous chondrite. What made the meteorite exceptional was how quickly it was recovered, allowing researchers to study those compounds before prolonged exposure to Earth's environment could contaminate the sample.

"One of the big surprises for me when we analyzed a small chip of the Hillsborough meteorite was the complexity of amino acids and other organic compounds," said Danny Glavin, senior scientist in the Astrobiology Analytical Laboratory at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, and co-author of the study.

Its diversity of amino acids and other organic compounds is, comparable to the Murchison meteorite, a nearly 100-kilogram carbonaceous chondrite that fell in Australia in 1969 and became the benchmark for extraterrestrial organic chemistry.

"It's just more proof that the chemical building blocks of life could have been delivered — and are still being delivered — to Earth today by these carbonaceous asteroid fragments,” said Glavin, who was a co-investigator on OSIRIS-REx, leading an international team that studied the organic composition of the samples delivered to Earth from asteroid Bennu in 2023.

Understanding the Hillsborough meteorite required expertise from multiple scientific disciplines.

Astronomers reconstructed the meteorite's journey through space, finding evidence that it may have originated from the Erigone asteroid family in the inner asteroid belt, home to the asteroid Donaldjohanson, which was visited in 2025 by NASA’s Lucy spacecraft. Mineralogists identified evidence of ancient brines preserved within microscopic fractures, while organic chemists analyzed the meteorite's inventory of amino acids and other organic compounds.

“Together, those complementary studies are helping scientists build one of the clearest pictures yet of how primitive asteroids such as the asteroid Erigone evolved chemically over billions of years,” said Jenniskens.

Researchers continue to study the Hillsborough meteorite, revealing new details about how water transformed primitive asteroids and shaped the early solar system.

By tracing the history of water on primitive asteroids, scientists are learning how water and the chemical ingredients for life were distributed throughout the early solar system.

"If you follow the water through the solar system, you're actually following life," Zolensky said. "Following the history of water through the solar system is an essential part of understanding the origin of life."

For more information on NASA’s astromaterials research and exploration, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/astromaterials

Karen Fox / Molly Wasser

Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 240-419-1732

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov

Victoria Segovia

NASA’s Johnson Space Center, Houston 281-483-5111

victoria.segovia@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery from a freshly recovered meteorite that provides new insights into ancient water, chemical evolution of asteroids, and the origins of life in the early solar system. This research has broad implications for understanding the solar system's history and the distribution of life's building blocks, benefiting the general public's knowledge of space and astrobiology. The article is focused and detailed, describing the meteorite's recovery, analysis, and scientific findings.
articles.personality_title: Fresh meteorite find reveals ancient water and organic clues from early asteroids
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On July 16, 2024, a meteorite fell to Earth in New Jersey. Scientists were eager to study it quickly because such space rocks hold important clues about the early solar system, including how water and life’s building blocks formed.

**What happened** – An amateur astronomer spotted the meteorite right after it landed and carefully collected its fragments to keep them clean. Cameras tracked the meteorite’s bright path through the sky, helping scientists figure out where it came from. NASA researchers studied the rock, named Hillsborough, and discovered it contains salty water traces and a rich mix of organic chemicals, including amino acids.

**Impact** – This meteorite is special because it was recovered so fast that delicate materials didn’t get damaged by Earth’s environment. It belongs to a rare group of space rocks called CM carbonaceous chondrites, which carry some of the oldest materials in the solar system. The salty water inside shows that ancient fluids changed the asteroid’s minerals billions of years ago. These findings help scientists understand how water and organic compounds spread through space, which is key to learning how life’s ingredients arrived on Earth.

**What's next step** – Researchers will keep studying the Hillsborough meteorite to learn more about how water shaped early asteroids. They also want to compare it with samples brought back by recent space missions to see how different asteroids evolved. This work will deepen our knowledge of solar system history and the origins of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The quickly recovered Hillsborough meteorite offers fresh evidence of ancient salty water and complex organic molecules that help explain how life’s ingredients formed in the early solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une météorite fraîche révèle de l'eau ancienne et des indices organiques des premiers astéroïdes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 16 juillet 2024, une météorite est tombée sur le New Jersey. Les scientifiques voulaient l'étudier rapidement, car ces roches spatiales contiennent des indices importants sur le système solaire primitif, notamment sur la formation de l'eau et des éléments essentiels à la vie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un astronome amateur a repéré la météorite juste après sa chute et a soigneusement collecté ses fragments pour les garder propres. Des caméras ont suivi sa trajectoire lumineuse dans le ciel, aidant les scientifiques à déterminer son origine. Les chercheurs de la NASA ont étudié la roche, nommée Hillsborough, et ont découvert qu'elle contenait des traces d'eau salée et un mélange riche de composés organiques, dont des acides aminés.

**Impact** – Cette météorite est spéciale car elle a été récupérée très rapidement, ce qui a permis de préserver des matériaux fragiles avant qu'ils ne soient altérés par l'environnement terrestre. Elle appartient à un groupe rare de roches spatiales appelées chondrites carbonées CM, qui contiennent certains des matériaux les plus anciens du système solaire. L'eau salée montre que des fluides anciens ont modifié les minéraux de l'astéroïde il y a des milliards d'années. Ces découvertes aident à comprendre comment l'eau et les composés organiques se sont répandus dans l'espace, ce qui est essentiel pour expliquer comment les ingrédients de la vie sont arrivés sur Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d'étudier la météorite Hillsborough pour mieux comprendre comment l'eau a façonné les premiers astéroïdes. Ils veulent aussi la comparer avec des échantillons ramenés par des missions spatiales récentes pour voir comment différents astéroïdes ont évolué. Ces travaux approfondiront nos connaissances sur l'histoire du système solaire et l'origine de la vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La météorite Hillsborough, récupérée rapidement, offre de nouvelles preuves d'eau salée ancienne et de molécules organiques complexes qui expliquent la formation des ingrédients de la vie dans le système solaire primitif.
articles.personality_title_es: Meteorito recién encontrado revela agua antigua y pistas orgánicas de asteroides primitivos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 16 de julio de 2024, un meteorito cayó en Nueva Jersey. Los científicos querían estudiarlo rápido porque estas rocas espaciales guardan pistas importantes sobre el sistema solar temprano, incluyendo cómo se formaron el agua y los ingredientes de la vida.

**Qué pasó** – Un astrónomo aficionado vio el meteorito justo después de que cayó y recogió sus fragmentos con cuidado para mantenerlos limpios. Cámaras registraron su paso brillante por el cielo, ayudando a los científicos a descubrir de dónde venía. Investigadores de la NASA estudiaron la roca, llamada Hillsborough, y encontraron que contiene rastros de agua salada y una mezcla rica en compuestos orgánicos, como aminoácidos.

**Impacto** – Este meteorito es especial porque se recuperó muy rápido, lo que permitió conservar materiales delicados antes de que el ambiente terrestre los dañara. Pertenece a un grupo raro de rocas espaciales llamadas condritas carbonosas CM, que contienen algunos de los materiales más antiguos del sistema solar. El agua salada muestra que fluidos antiguos cambiaron los minerales del asteroide hace miles de millones de años. Estos hallazgos ayudan a entender cómo el agua y los compuestos orgánicos se distribuyeron en el espacio, algo clave para explicar cómo llegaron a la Tierra los ingredientes para la vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando el meteorito Hillsborough para aprender más sobre cómo el agua transformó los primeros asteroides. También quieren compararlo con muestras traídas por misiones espaciales recientes para ver cómo evolucionaron distintos asteroides. Este trabajo ampliará nuestro conocimiento sobre la historia del sistema solar y el origen de la vida.

**Resumen en una frase** – El meteorito Hillsborough, recuperado rápidamente, ofrece nuevas pruebas de agua salada antigua y moléculas orgánicas complejas que ayudan a explicar cómo se formaron los ingredientes de la vida en el sistema solar temprano.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing meteorite fragment gently resting on soft earth, surrounded by delicate crystalline mineral patterns and shimmering sodium-rich veins visible beneath its surface, with a faint, ethereal trail of a fiery fireball arcing across a dusky sky above a calm New Jersey landscape.

articles.title: How NASA’s Artemis III Lander Test Will Pave Way for Moon Landings
articles.summary: Before Artemis astronauts land on the Moon’s surface in 2028, NASA will conduct the Artemis III demonstration mission in 2027, allowing teams on Earth and in orbit to practice rendezvous and docking operations between commercial human landing systems and the Orion spacecraft. Data from that mission, along with future uncrewed demonstration missions at the Moon, […]
articles.content:
Before Artemis astronauts land on the Moon’s surface in 2028, NASA will conduct the Artemis III demonstration mission in 2027, allowing teams on Earth and in orbit to practice rendezvous and docking operations between commercial human landing systems and the Orion spacecraft. Data from that mission, along with future uncrewed demonstration missions at the Moon, will support astronaut safety and mission success for crewed lunar landings.

NASA is working with two American companies to develop the human landing systems that will safely transport astronauts from lunar orbit to the Moon’s surface and back for future Artemis missions. For Artemis III, both SpaceX and Blue Origin will fly test versions, or test articles, of the crewed landers that will be used for future Moon landings. The lander test articles will launch by commercial rockets, while the Artemis III crew will launch to low Earth orbit in Orion atop the agency’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket.

Stage setting for crewed flights

NASA and the human landing system providers have been working closely together to plan and determine capabilities for the Artemis III mission. With missions fast approaching, both SpaceX and Blue Origin are optimizing hardware availability and capability. SpaceX plans to use the company’s latest version of Starship and basis of the future Starship HLS, called Version 3, while Blue Origin will test their planned HLS crew cabin, allowing each company to apply lessons learned prior to uncrewed and crewed missions on the Moon.

“Each human landing system provider has taken a different approach to the Artemis III mission,” said Steve Creech, program manager, Human Landing System Program, NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. “Ultimately, SpaceX and Blue Origin have put forward a list of aggressive objectives and goals intended to complement upcoming uncrewed demonstration missions at the Moon so that we can gain both understanding and confidence in the spacecraft and launch vehicles prior to a crewed landing. The lander prototype designs will inform future development efforts and will continue to mature over the next year.”

For the Artemis III mission, the Blue Moon test lander will be based on Blue Origin’s current architecture for its Mark 2 crew lander, incorporating all the major avionics and flight software and control systems to ensure flight operations from this demonstration mission can directly translate to crewed lunar flights. Up to two crew members, donning orange Orion crew survival system suits, will open the hatch to enter the Blue Origin test lander. The production hardware must incorporate many of the same systems and subsystems, including an Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS), a crew cabin, and avionics.

These artist’s concepts depict Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 human landing system test article in Earth orbit and docked with NASA’s Orion spacecraft as part of NASA’s upcoming Artemis III demonstration mission in low Earth orbit. Blue Origin These artist’s concepts depict Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 human landing system test article in Earth orbit and docked with NASA’s Orion spacecraft as part of NASA’s upcoming Artemis III demonstration mission in low Earth orbit. Blue Origin

The Blue Origin lander also will fly with an instrumented lunar surface spacesuit mass simulator. Like the suited “Moonikin” manikin that flew aboard Orion during the uncrewed Artemis I test flight, the low-fidelity spacesuit mass simulator will provide real-time feedback about the environment within the Blue Moon crew cabin.

SpaceX’s Starship lander test article will use a Starship Version 3, currently in production and testing, with an added docking system installed on the nose of the 171-foot (52-m) spacecraft, enabling NASA and SpaceX to evaluate how the entire integrated stack of Orion and the Starship test lander interact. NASA and SpaceX are identifying controllability and communications tests for the Artemis III mission. Astronauts will not enter the Starship test lander during Artemis III.

These artist’s concepts depict NASA’s Orion spacecraft and SpaceX’s Starship human landing system test article during docking operations as part of NASA’s upcoming Artemis III demonstration mission in low Earth orbit. SpaceX These artist’s concepts depict NASA’s Orion spacecraft and SpaceX’s Starship human landing system test article during docking operations as part of NASA’s upcoming Artemis III demonstration mission in low Earth orbit. SpaceX

Launch cadence

NASA, SpaceX, and Blue Origin will launch three of the world’s most powerful rockets within a short timeframe of one another, exercising ground processing, launch crews, and facilities as well as control centers, networking, and data exchange at key sites across the country during two separate, back-to-back rendezvous and docking maneuvers between Orion and the lander test articles, before a safe splashdown of the Artemis III crew in Orion.

“Artemis III will be a highly choreographed dance with a demanding launch sequence across multiple launch pads and equally demanding mission operations for our ground and flight crews, making it one of the most complex and ambitious missions NASA has ever undertaken,” said Jeremy Parsons, Artemis program manager. “The demonstration mission will set the stage before our next giant leap. NASA’s expertise in systems engineering and integration, as well as launch and mission operations in low Earth orbit, will bring the mission together.”

For future crewed missions to the Moon, NASA and one of the commercial lander partners will execute a “dual launch campaign,” prepositioning the lander in orbit to await a crewed Orion, launched on SLS. Launching the three rockets in succession of one another for Artemis III offers a unique opportunity to practice launch processing and operations.

Blue Origin’s lander test article is planned to launch first and will be able to loiter in space for up to 30 days, allowing for checkouts in orbit prior to the launch of SLS and Orion from Launch Complex 39B at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. The Blue Origin test article will launch at a set trajectory to meet a designated “parking” orbit for these systems checks.

Artemis III will be a highly choreographed dance with a demanding launch sequence across multiple launch pads and equally demanding mission operations for our ground and flight crews, making it one of the most complex and ambitious missions NASA has ever undertaken. Jeremy Parsons Artemis program manager

Following the completion of Blue Origin’s rendezvous and docking operations testing and the Artemis III crewed launch on SLS, SpaceX will launch its Starship lander test article to rendezvous with Orion and its crew for its phase of on-orbit testing.

Throughout the Artemis III mission, Orion will fly in a circular orbit. All three rockets will have more launch opportunities than are available for a lunar mission and will be able to reach the designated mission altitude in a single launch.

Docking operations

During docking and undocking operations, Orion and the Artemis III crew will use the lander test articles as the targets, while Orion will operate as the chaser spacecraft. This is the same configuration planned for future crew landing mission to the Moon.

NASA will ensure both test landers are mission ready and crew safe prior to Artemis III. These verifications will be based on functional and performance requirements for the test lander designs and hazard controls for hardware and software, ensuring the Artemis III astronauts inside Orion are safe throughout both docking phases of the mission.

SpaceX and Blue Origin have already tested their docking capabilities for their respective landers on the ground. SpaceX’s docking capability was qualified in 2023; Blue Origin conducted development ground testing on its pressurized docking system earlier this year.

A key difference between the docking capabilities of both lander test articles will be the location of docking. Orion will dock along the side of the Blue Moon test lander, adjacent to the crew cabin. Later, Orion will dock nose-to-nose with the giant SpaceX test lander.

Software testing between spacecrafts will help demonstrate that the commercial human landing system prototypes and Orion can meet at a precise time and location in space. When Orion docks with the Blue Moon test lander, the Orion spacecraft’s software will control the docked spacecraft. Meanwhile, the SpaceX test article will control the docked spacecraft for the second portion of the mission. During the docking phases, teams with NASA and the commercial partners will be able to test hardware and software interoperability, as well as dynamics of how the integrated lander-Orion spacecraft moves in space.

Through the Artemis program, NASA will send astronauts to explore the Moon for scientific discovery, economic benefits, and to build the foundation for the first crewed missions to Mars – for the benefit of all.

News Media Contact

AmberJacobson

Headquarters, Washington

240.298.1832

amber.c.jacobson@nasa.gov

Corinne Beckinger

Marshall Space Flight Center, Huntsville, Ala.

256-544-0034

corinne.m.beckinger@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article details NASA's Artemis III mission preparations involving test flights of human landing systems by SpaceX and Blue Origin, which represent significant progress toward crewed Moon landings. This has broad significance for space exploration, scientific discovery, and future human missions to Mars, with detailed context on the mission's objectives and technological advancements.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis III mission tests new Moon landers in space before 2028 landing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA plans to send astronauts to the Moon’s surface in 2028 as part of its Artemis program. To prepare, NASA will test new human landing systems designed to carry astronauts safely from lunar orbit to the Moon and back.

**What happened** – In 2027, NASA will conduct the Artemis III demonstration mission. This mission will test two different Moon landers built by SpaceX and Blue Origin. These landers will launch separately into space, where they will practice docking with NASA’s Orion spacecraft. Astronauts will fly aboard Orion, but will only enter the Blue Origin lander during tests. SpaceX’s Starship lander will be tested without astronauts onboard.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it allows NASA and its partners to practice and check how well the landers and Orion work together in space. Testing docking and control systems ahead of time helps ensure astronaut safety and mission success for future Moon landings. The mission also tests launching three powerful rockets in a short time, preparing ground teams for complex space operations.

**What's next step** – After these tests, NASA will use the lessons learned to improve the landers and prepare for crewed Moon landings starting with Artemis III in 2028. The data will help make sure systems are ready and safe for astronauts. This mission also builds experience for future missions that could take humans to Mars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis III mission will test new Moon landers in space to prepare for safe astronaut landings on the Moon in 2028 and beyond.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis III de la NASA teste de nouveaux atterrisseurs lunaires avant l’atterrissage de 2028
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA prévoit d’envoyer des astronautes sur la surface de la Lune en 2028 dans le cadre de son programme Artemis. Pour se préparer, la NASA va tester de nouveaux systèmes d’atterrissage humain conçus pour transporter les astronautes en toute sécurité de l’orbite lunaire à la surface de la Lune et retour.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2027, la NASA réalisera la mission de démonstration Artemis III. Cette mission testera deux atterrisseurs lunaires différents construits par SpaceX et Blue Origin. Ces atterrisseurs seront lancés séparément dans l’espace, où ils pratiqueront l’amarrage avec le vaisseau Orion de la NASA. Des astronautes voleront à bord d’Orion, mais ne monteront que dans l’atterrisseur de Blue Origin pendant les tests. L’atterrisseur Starship de SpaceX sera testé sans astronautes à bord.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car elle permet à la NASA et à ses partenaires de s’entraîner et de vérifier le bon fonctionnement des atterrisseurs et d’Orion dans l’espace. Tester les systèmes d’amarrage et de contrôle à l’avance aide à garantir la sécurité des astronautes et la réussite des futures missions lunaires. La mission teste aussi le lancement de trois fusées puissantes en peu de temps, préparant les équipes au sol pour des opérations spatiales complexes.

**Prochaines étapes** – Après ces tests, la NASA utilisera les enseignements pour améliorer les atterrisseurs et préparer les atterrissages habités dès Artemis III en 2028. Les données aideront à s’assurer que les systèmes sont prêts et sûrs pour les astronautes. Cette mission prépare aussi les futures missions humaines vers Mars.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis III de la NASA testera de nouveaux atterrisseurs lunaires dans l’espace pour préparer des atterrissages sûrs des astronautes sur la Lune en 2028 et au-delà.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis III de la NASA prueba nuevos módulos lunares antes del aterrizaje de 2028
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA planea enviar astronautas a la superficie de la Luna en 2028 como parte de su programa Artemis. Para prepararse, la NASA probará nuevos sistemas de aterrizaje humano diseñados para transportar a los astronautas de manera segura desde la órbita lunar hasta la Luna y de regreso.

**Qué pasó** – En 2027, la NASA realizará la misión de demostración Artemis III. Esta misión probará dos módulos lunares diferentes construidos por SpaceX y Blue Origin. Estos módulos se lanzarán por separado al espacio, donde practicarán el acoplamiento con la nave Orion de la NASA. Los astronautas volarán en Orion, pero solo entrarán en el módulo de Blue Origin durante las pruebas. El módulo Starship de SpaceX será probado sin astronautas a bordo.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque permite a la NASA y sus socios practicar y verificar cómo funcionan juntos los módulos y Orion en el espacio. Probar los sistemas de acoplamiento y control con anticipación ayuda a garantizar la seguridad de los astronautas y el éxito de futuras misiones lunares. También se probará el lanzamiento de tres cohetes potentes en poco tiempo, preparando a los equipos en tierra para operaciones espaciales complejas.

**Próximos pasos** – Después de estas pruebas, la NASA usará lo aprendido para mejorar los módulos y prepararse para aterrizajes tripulados a partir de Artemis III en 2028. Los datos ayudarán a asegurar que los sistemas estén listos y sean seguros para los astronautas. Esta misión también prepara el camino para futuras misiones humanas a Marte.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión Artemis III de la NASA probará nuevos módulos lunares en el espacio para preparar aterrizajes seguros de astronautas en la Luna en 2028 y más allá.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two stylized spacecraft—one sleek and cylindrical with a nose docking port, the other boxy with a hatch and crew cabin—gracefully approaching each other in calm low Earth orbit above a softly glowing, crescent Earth, symbolizing collaboration and precise rendezvous in space exploration.

articles.title: Astronomers just found four hidden white dwarf stars near Earth
articles.summary: Four nearby white dwarf stars have been discovered hiding in plain sight beside brighter red dwarf companions. Hubble's ultraviolet observations finally revealed the long-hidden stellar remnants, including one just 25 light-years away that took nearly three decades to confirm. The findings match long-standing predictions and suggest our corner of the galaxy may contain many more undiscovered white dwarf binaries.
articles.content:
Astronomers have directly confirmed four previously hidden white dwarf stars in nearby binary systems. Every system lies within 65 light-years of Earth, and one contains the ninth closest known white dwarf to the Sun.

The discoveries were made by researchers at the University of Warwick and the University of Colorado Boulder. Their findings were published in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society (MNRAS).

White Dwarfs Hidden by Brighter Companions

Each white dwarf orbits alongside a red dwarf star. Because the red dwarfs appear larger and brighter, the systems looked like they contained only a single star when viewed in visible light.

The new observations revealed that all four nearby red dwarfs were concealing white dwarf companions.

First author, Dr. Mairi O'Brien, Research Fellow, University of Warwick said: "Nearby isolated white dwarfs are usually easy to find, but we couldn't see these four stars directly in visible wavelengths because their red dwarf companions were drowning out their light. It's a reminder that even in our own cosmic neighborhood, we can still find surprises if we look in the right way, at the right wavelengths."

Astronomers have spent decades carefully cataloging stars near the Sun, yet white dwarfs in systems like these remain difficult to detect. The four systems attracted attention because their visible stars showed a pronounced radial wobble.

This motion occurs when a star moves slightly toward and away from Earth as an unseen, massive object pulls on it while orbiting. The wobble suggested that each red dwarf had a hidden companion.

Hubble Ultraviolet Data Reveals the Stars

The researchers used ultraviolet spectrograph data from the Hubble Space Telescope to examine the four systems in greater detail.

White dwarfs are generally much easier to recognize in ultraviolet light. Red dwarfs, however, can produce powerful flares that imitate the ultraviolet signal of a white dwarf, making confirmation more difficult.

To separate the true signals from the effects of stellar flaring, the team developed specialized calibration methods. This analysis officially confirmed that all four systems contained white dwarf stars.

A White Dwarf Hidden for 27 Years

One of the systems, G 203-47, was especially puzzling. Although it is located only 25 light-years away, astronomers needed 27 years after first detecting its radial wobble to identify the hidden white dwarf.

The object is now recognized as the ninth closest white dwarf to the Sun.

G 203-47 also behaves differently from similar binary systems. Its red dwarf takes more than 100 days to complete one rotation, even though it circles the white dwarf every 14.9 days.

Under normal circumstances, the gravitational interaction between two closely orbiting stars would be expected to synchronize their motion through tidal locking. The Moon and Earth provide a familiar example, since the same side of the Moon continually faces Earth.

In G 203-47, however, the red dwarf rotates far too slowly to be synchronized with its orbit.

Coauthor Dr. David Wilson, Research Associate, University of Colorado Boulder, said: "What's fascinating is that G 203-47 shouldn't be rotating this slowly if it formed the same way as similar systems. This suggests that these binaries have had very different evolutionary histories. Some underwent violent, prolonged interactions early on that locked them tidally. Others, like G 203-47, experienced gentler, briefer encounters that left them in this unusual state."

Clues About Binary Star Evolution

The unusual rotation of G 203-47 suggests that not all white dwarf and red dwarf pairs developed through the same process.

Some systems may have experienced long and intense interactions early in their histories, causing the stars to become tidally locked. Others may have interacted for a shorter period and with less force, leaving their rotations unsynchronized.

The four discoveries have also allowed astronomers to revise the census of white dwarfs within 20 parsecs (65 light-years).

Previous population models predicted that roughly 4 to 5 closely orbiting white dwarf and red dwarf systems should exist in this region. The researchers identified exactly four, closely matching those theoretical estimates.

More Hidden White Dwarfs May Be Nearby

The discoveries may not complete the picture. Most nearby red dwarfs have not yet been systematically examined for concealed white dwarf companions.

Professor Pier-Emmanuel Tremblay, Astronomy and Astrophysics Group, University of Warwick, said: "Only about 30 percent of red dwarfs within 20 parsecs have been systematically surveyed for hidden white dwarf companions. We think there could be as many as 9 or 10 additional binary systems in our local stellar environment that we haven't found yet. If we put more targeted effort into observing red dwarfs, perhaps we will find more surprises like this."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant astronomical discovery of four previously hidden white dwarf stars near Earth, confirming long-standing predictions and improving understanding of nearby stellar populations. This has broad scientific significance for our knowledge of the galaxy and stellar evolution, with detailed context and implications provided.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers find four hidden white dwarf stars near Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – White dwarf stars are small, dense remnants of stars that have used up their fuel. They often orbit other stars, but some are hard to spot when paired with brighter stars nearby.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered four white dwarf stars close to Earth that were hidden by brighter red dwarf stars. These stars are all within 65 light-years of us. Using the Hubble Space Telescope’s ultraviolet data, researchers confirmed the white dwarfs, including one only 25 light-years away that took 27 years to identify.

**Impact** – This discovery matches predictions about how many such pairs should exist near us. It shows that many white dwarfs may still be hidden because their red dwarf companions outshine them in visible light. One system, called G 203-47, also behaves differently by having its red dwarf rotate more slowly than expected, giving clues about how these star pairs evolve.

**What's next step** – Astronomers plan to study more red dwarfs nearby to find additional hidden white dwarfs. So far, only 30% of red dwarfs within 65 light-years have been checked, and there could be up to 10 more hidden pairs waiting to be discovered.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using ultraviolet observations, astronomers uncovered four hidden white dwarf stars near Earth, improving our understanding of nearby star systems and their evolution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes découvrent quatre naines blanches cachées près de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les naines blanches sont des étoiles très denses en fin de vie. Elles tournent souvent autour d’autres étoiles, mais sont difficiles à voir quand elles sont proches d’étoiles plus brillantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert quatre naines blanches près de la Terre, cachées par des étoiles naines rouges plus lumineuses. Ces étoiles se trouvent toutes à moins de 65 années-lumière de nous. Grâce aux observations en ultraviolet du télescope spatial Hubble, ils ont confirmé ces naines blanches, dont une située à seulement 25 années-lumière et identifiée après 27 ans d’observation.

**Impact** – Cette découverte confirme les prévisions sur le nombre de ces couples d’étoiles proches de nous. Elle montre que beaucoup de naines blanches restent cachées car leurs compagnons naines rouges brillent plus en lumière visible. Un système appelé G 203-47 se distingue aussi par la rotation lente de sa naine rouge, ce qui donne des indices sur l’évolution de ces couples d’étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les astronomes vont étudier plus de naines rouges proches pour trouver d’autres naines blanches cachées. Jusqu’à présent, seulement 30 % des naines rouges dans un rayon de 65 années-lumière ont été examinées, et jusqu’à 10 autres couples pourraient être découverts.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce aux observations en ultraviolet, les astronomes ont découvert quatre naines blanches cachées près de la Terre, améliorant notre compréhension des systèmes stellaires proches et de leur évolution.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos descubren cuatro enanas blancas ocultas cerca de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enanas blancas son estrellas pequeñas y densas que han agotado su combustible. A menudo orbitan otras estrellas, pero pueden ser difíciles de ver cuando están junto a estrellas más brillantes.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos encontraron cuatro enanas blancas cerca de la Tierra que estaban ocultas por estrellas enanas rojas más brillantes. Todas están a menos de 65 años luz de nosotros. Usando datos ultravioletas del telescopio espacial Hubble, confirmaron estas enanas blancas, incluyendo una que está a solo 25 años luz y que tardaron 27 años en identificar.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento coincide con predicciones sobre cuántos pares de estrellas así deberían existir cerca. Muestra que muchas enanas blancas pueden estar ocultas porque sus compañeras enanas rojas son más brillantes en luz visible. Un sistema llamado G 203-47 tiene una enana roja que gira más lento de lo esperado, lo que da pistas sobre la evolución de estas estrellas.

**Próximo paso** – Los astrónomos planean estudiar más enanas rojas cercanas para encontrar más enanas blancas ocultas. Hasta ahora, solo se ha examinado el 30 % de las enanas rojas dentro de 65 años luz, y podrían existir hasta 10 pares ocultos más.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando observaciones ultravioletas, los astrónomos descubrieron cuatro enanas blancas ocultas cerca de la Tierra, mejorando nuestro conocimiento sobre sistemas estelares cercanos y su evolución.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined glowing orbs—one bright white and small, the other larger and soft red—circling each other gently against a simple dark space background, with subtle ripples around them symbolizing their gravitational dance and hidden companionship.
